{{Short description|Protein-coding gene in the species Homo sapiens}}
{{cs1 config|name-list-style=vanc|display-authors=6}}
{{Infobox gene|Name=Endoplasmic reticulum aminopeptidase 1 (ERAP1)|AltNames=ERAP1, ARTS-1, Adipocyte-derived leucine aminopeptidase (A-LAP), Aminopeptidase PILS, Puromycin-insensitive leucyl-specific aminopeptidase (PILS-AP), Type 1 tumor necrosis factor receptor shedding aminopeptidase regulator|Symbol=ERAP1|AltSymbols=RTS-1, A-LAP, PILS-AP, KIAA0525, ERAAP1|EntrezGene=51752|HGNCid=18173|OMIM=606832|PDB=2YD0, 3MDJ, 3QNF, 3RJO|RefSeq=NM_016442.5|UniProt=Q9NZ08|ECnumber=3.4.11|Chromosome=5|Arm=q|Band=15|LocusSupplementaryData=Chr.5 5q15}}

'''Endoplasmic reticulum aminopeptidase 1''' ('''ERAP1''') is an [[enzyme]] that in humans is encoded by the ''ERAP1'' [[gene]]. This M1 zinc [[aminopeptidase]] is involved in the [[antigen processing]] and presentation pathway. ERAP1 is mainly located in the [[endoplasmic reticulum]] (ER), where it trims peptides at their [[N-terminus]], adapting them for presentation by [[MHC class I|MHC class I molecules]] (MHC-I).<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Shastri N, Nagarajan N, Lind KC, Kanaseki T | title = Monitoring peptide processing for MHC class I molecules in the endoplasmic reticulum | journal = Current Opinion in Immunology | volume = 26 | pages = 123–127 | date = February 2014 | pmid = 24556408 | pmc = 3932006 | doi = 10.1016/j.coi.2013.11.006 }}</ref>

== Nomenclature ==
Historical names of ERAP1, [<nowiki/>[[HUGO Gene Nomenclature Committee]]]:

* Aminopeptidase regulator of tumour necrosis factor receptor 1 (TNFR1) shedding (ARTS-1)
* Adipocyte-derived [[leucine]] aminopeptidase (A-LAP)
* Puromycin-insensitive leucyl-specific aminopeptidase (PILS-AP)
* KIAA0525
* In mice, ER aminopeptidase associated with antigen processing (ERAAP)

== Biology / Functions ==
Efficient presentation of antigenic peptides by MHC class I molecules provides the key signal for [[Adaptive immune system|adaptive immune responses]] by [[Cytotoxic T cell|cytotoxic (CD8<sup>+</sup>) T lymphocytes]]. In the "endogenous" [[antigen presentation]] pathway, proteins synthesized by cells undergo cytosolic degradation and some of their peptide fragments are transported to the ER, where suitable-length peptides are loaded onto MHC class I molecules. In the ER, ERAP1 shortens longer peptides to the optimal length for stable binding onto MHC class I molecules (Figure 1). ERAP1, like other APP components, is induced by [[interferon gamma]], a cytokine inducing antigen presentation.<ref name="Saric_2002">{{cite journal | vauthors = Saric T, Chang SC, Hattori A, York IA, Markant S, Rock KL, Tsujimoto M, Goldberg AL | title = An IFN-gamma-induced aminopeptidase in the ER, ERAP1, trims precursors to MHC class I-presented peptides | journal = Nature Immunology | volume = 3 | issue = 12 | pages = 1169–1176 | date = December 2002 | pmid = 12436109 | doi = 10.1038/ni859 }}</ref> ERAP1 preferentially trims N-extended substrates of 9-16 residues to optimally sized 8-10 residue peptides.<ref name="Chang_2005">{{cite journal | vauthors = Chang SC, Momburg F, Bhutani N, Goldberg AL | title = The ER aminopeptidase, ERAP1, trims precursors to lengths of MHC class I peptides by a "molecular ruler" mechanism | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 102 | issue = 47 | pages = 17107–17112 | date = November 2005 | pmid = 16286653 | pmc = 1287962 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.0500721102 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2005PNAS..10217107C }}</ref> This "molecular ruler" mechanism is unique to ERAP1. ERAP1 also functions in the presentation of extracellular antigens in the cross-presentation pathway. [[File:Figure 1 Scheme of ERAP1 role in antigen processing and presentation..tif|thumb|'''Figure 1.''' Scheme of ERAP1 role in antigen processing and presentation.|335x335px|left]]ERAP1 "trimming" modulates the peptide repertoire presented by MHC class I molecules and thereby shapes the adaptive immune response.<ref name="Saric_2002" /><ref name="Hammer_2006">{{cite journal | vauthors = Hammer GE, Gonzalez F, Champsaur M, Cado D, Shastri N | title = The aminopeptidase ERAAP shapes the peptide repertoire displayed by major histocompatibility complex class I molecules | journal = Nature Immunology | volume = 7 | issue = 1 | pages = 103–112 | date = January 2006 | pmid = 16299505 | doi = 10.1038/ni1286 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Serwold T, Gonzalez F, Kim J, Jacob R, Shastri N | title = ERAAP customizes peptides for MHC class I molecules in the endoplasmic reticulum | journal = Nature | volume = 419 | issue = 6906 | pages = 480–483 | date = October 2002 | pmid = 12368856 | doi = 10.1038/nature01074 | bibcode = 2002Natur.419..480S }}</ref> In murine models, ERAAP (the murine homologue of human ERAP1) deficiency results in a strong alteration and increased [[immunogenicity]] of the peptide repertoire presented by MHC-I.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hammer GE, Gonzalez F, James E, Nolla H, Shastri N | title = In the absence of aminopeptidase ERAAP, MHC class I molecules present many unstable and highly immunogenic peptides | journal = Nature Immunology | volume = 8 | issue = 1 | pages = 101–108 | date = January 2007 | pmid = 17128277 | doi = 10.1038/ni1409 }}</ref> Murine models with genetic deficiency for ERAAP have been instrumental for understanding the role of peptide trimming in the ER.<ref name="Hammer_2006" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Firat E, Saveanu L, Aichele P, Staeheli P, Huai J, Gaedicke S, Nil A, Besin G, Kanzler B, van Endert P, Niedermann G | title = The role of endoplasmic reticulum-associated aminopeptidase 1 in immunity to infection and in cross-presentation | journal = Journal of Immunology | volume = 178 | issue = 4 | pages = 2241–2248 | date = February 2007 | pmid = 17277129 | doi = 10.4049/jimmunol.178.4.2241 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = York IA, Brehm MA, Zendzian S, Towne CF, Rock KL | title = Endoplasmic reticulum aminopeptidase 1 (ERAP1) trims MHC class I-presented peptides in vivo and plays an important role in immunodominance | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 103 | issue = 24 | pages = 9202–9207 | date = June 2006 | pmid = 16754858 | pmc = 1482590 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.0603095103 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2006PNAS..103.9202Y }}</ref>

Besides peptide trimming in the ER, ERAP1 has been proposed to perform additional functions depending on its location. ERAP1 can be secreted into the extracellular space in response to inflammatory stimuli, which can lead to the activation of immune cells, such as [[macrophage]]s or [[natural killer cell]]s, and enhanced expression of [[Inflammatory cytokine|pro-inflammatory cytokines]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Tsujimoto M, Aoki K, Ohnishi A, Goto Y | title = Endoplasmic Reticulum Aminopeptidase 1 beyond Antigenic Peptide-Processing Enzyme in the Endoplasmic Reticulum | journal = Biological & Pharmaceutical Bulletin | volume = 43 | issue = 2 | pages = 207–214 | date = 2020-02-01 | pmid = 32009107 | doi = 10.1248/bpb.b19-00857 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

== Genetics / Clinical significance ==

=== Gene / location ===
The ''ERAP1'' gene ([https://www.genenames.org/tools/search/#!/genes?query=ERAP1 HGNC: 18173]) is located at the long arm of [[chromosome 5]] (''5q15''). The gene is ~47Kb in length<ref>{{Cite web |title=ERAP1 endoplasmic reticulum aminopeptidase 1 [Homo sapiens (human)] |url=https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gene?Db=gene&Cmd=DetailsSearch&Term=51752}}</ref> and contains 20 exons and 19 introns,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hattori A, Matsumoto K, Mizutani S, Tsujimoto M | title = Genomic organization of the human adipocyte-derived leucine aminopeptidase gene and its relationship to the placental leucine aminopeptidase/oxytocinase gene | journal = Journal of Biochemistry | volume = 130 | issue = 2 | pages = 235–241 | date = August 2001 | pmid = 11481040 | doi = 10.1093/oxfordjournals.jbchem.a002977 | doi-access = free }}</ref> which encode 9 different splice variants. The coding sequence shows a high degree of conservation among placental mammals (>80% identity). The sequences of 227 ''ERAP1'' orthologs identified in approximately 200 species are available.<ref>{{Cite web |title=ERAP1 ENSG00000164307 |url=https://www.ensembl.org/Homo_sapiens/Gene/Summary?g=ENSG00000164307;r=5:96760810-96808100}}</ref>

=== SNPs ===
ERAP1 is a [[Gene polymorphism|polymorphic gene]] that has many [[Single-nucleotide polymorphism|single nucleotide variants]] (SNVs) including several common [[Missense mutation|missense variants]] that alter the ERAP1 amino acid sequence. The various combinations of common SNVs in ''ERAP1'' organize into distinct [[haplotype]]s that encode different protein isoforms often referred to as "[[Allotype (immunology)|allotypes]]".<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Tran TM, Colbert RA | title = Endoplasmic reticulum aminopeptidase 1 and rheumatic disease: functional variation | journal = Current Opinion in Rheumatology | volume = 27 | issue = 4 | pages = 357–363 | date = July 2015 | pmid = 26002027 | pmc = 4495904 | doi = 10.1097/BOR.0000000000000188 }}</ref> The allotypes of ERAP1 can broadly be categorized based on their enzymatic activity ranging from "high" to "low".<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Reeves E, James E | title = The role of polymorphic ERAP1 in autoinflammatory disease | journal = Bioscience Reports | volume = 38 | issue = 4 | date = September 2018 | article-number = BSR20171503 | pmid = 30054427 | doi = 10.1042/BSR20171503 | pmc = 6131210 }}</ref><ref name="Hutchinson_2021">{{cite journal | vauthors = Hutchinson JP, Temponeras I, Kuiper J, Cortes A, Korczynska J, Kitchen S, Stratikos E | title = Common allotypes of ER aminopeptidase 1 have substrate-dependent and highly variable enzymatic properties | journal = The Journal of Biological Chemistry | volume = 296 | article-number = 100443 | date = January 2021 | pmid = 33617882 | pmc = 8024916 | doi = 10.1016/j.jbc.2021.100443 | doi-access = free }}</ref> The enzymatic activity of ERAP1 is dependent on substrate recognition of the peptide, seen by the trimming efficiency of specific peptide substrates varying significantly for a given allotype. As is the case for allotype 10, a poor trimming allotype, shown to be tenfold less active compared to the ancestral allotype in hydrolysis of the substrate l-leucine-7-amido-4-methylcoumarin (Leu-AMC).<ref name="Hutchinson_2021" />

=== Disease association ===
Genetic variants and haplotypes (i.e., allotypes) of ''ERAP1'' have been associated with a wide variety of inflammatory conditions, infectious diseases, and cancer. In particular, ''ERAP1'' is a major risk gene identified in [[Genome-wide association study|genome-wide association studies]] of MHC-I associated inflammatory conditions (or "MHC-I-opathies"), including [[Ankylosing spondylitis|Ankylosing Spondylitis]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Evans DM, Spencer CC, Pointon JJ, Su Z, Harvey D, Kochan G, Oppermann U, Dilthey A, Pirinen M, Stone MA, Appleton L, Moutsianas L, Leslie S, Wordsworth T, Kenna TJ, Karaderi T, Thomas GP, Ward MM, Weisman MH, Farrar C, Bradbury LA, Danoy P, Inman RD, Maksymowych W, Gladman D, Rahman P, Morgan A, Marzo-Ortega H, Bowness P, Gaffney K, Gaston JS, Smith M, Bruges-Armas J, Couto AR, Sorrentino R, Paladini F, Ferreira MA, Xu H, Liu Y, Jiang L, Lopez-Larrea C, Díaz-Peña R, López-Vázquez A, Zayats T, Band G, Bellenguez C, Blackburn H, Blackwell JM, Bramon E, Bumpstead SJ, Casas JP, Corvin A, Craddock N, Deloukas P, Dronov S, Duncanson A, Edkins S, Freeman C, Gillman M, Gray E, Gwilliam R, Hammond N, Hunt SE, Jankowski J, Jayakumar A, Langford C, Liddle J, Markus HS, Mathew CG, McCann OT, McCarthy MI, Palmer CN, Peltonen L, Plomin R, Potter SC, Rautanen A, Ravindrarajah R, Ricketts M, Samani N, Sawcer SJ, Strange A, Trembath RC, Viswanathan AC, Waller M, Weston P, Whittaker P, Widaa S, Wood NW, McVean G, Reveille JD, Wordsworth BP, Brown MA, Donnelly P | title = Interaction between ERAP1 and HLA-B27 in ankylosing spondylitis implicates peptide handling in the mechanism for HLA-B27 in disease susceptibility | journal = Nature Genetics | volume = 43 | issue = 8 | pages = 761–767 | date = July 2011 | pmid = 21743469 | pmc = 3640413 | doi = 10.1038/ng.873 }}</ref> [[Behçet's disease|Bechet's disease]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kirino Y, Bertsias G, Ishigatsubo Y, Mizuki N, Tugal-Tutkun I, Seyahi E, Ozyazgan Y, Sacli FS, Erer B, Inoko H, Emrence Z, Cakar A, Abaci N, Ustek D, Satorius C, Ueda A, Takeno M, Kim Y, Wood GM, Ombrello MJ, Meguro A, Gül A, Remmers EF, Kastner DL | title = Genome-wide association analysis identifies new susceptibility loci for Behçet's disease and epistasis between HLA-B*51 and ERAP1 | journal = Nature Genetics | volume = 45 | issue = 2 | pages = 202–207 | date = February 2013 | pmid = 23291587 | pmc = 3810947 | doi = 10.1038/ng.2520 }}</ref> Birdshot [[Uveitis]],<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kuiper JJ, Setten JV, Devall M, Cretu-Stancu M, Hiddingh S, Ophoff RA, Missotten TO, Velthoven MV, Den Hollander AI, Hoyng CB, James E, Reeves E, Cordero-Coma M, Fonollosa A, Adán A, Martín J, Koeleman BP, Boer JH, Pulit SL, Márquez A, Radstake TR | title = Functionally distinct ERAP1 and ERAP2 are a hallmark of HLA-A29-(Birdshot) Uveitis | journal = Human Molecular Genetics | volume = 27 | issue = 24 | pages = 4333–4343 | date = December 2018 | pmid = 30215709 | pmc = 6276832 | doi = 10.1093/hmg/ddy319 }}</ref> and [[Psoriasis]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Strange A, Capon F, Spencer CC, Knight J, Weale ME, Allen MH, Barton A, Band G, Bellenguez C, Bergboer JG, Blackwell JM, Bramon E, Bumpstead SJ, Casas JP, Cork MJ, Corvin A, Deloukas P, Dilthey A, Duncanson A, Edkins S, Estivill X, Fitzgerald O, Freeman C, Giardina E, Gray E, Hofer A, Hüffmeier U, Hunt SE, Irvine AD, Jankowski J, Kirby B, Langford C, Lascorz J, Leman J, Leslie S, Mallbris L, Markus HS, Mathew CG, McLean WH, McManus R, Mössner R, Moutsianas L, Naluai AT, Nestle FO, Novelli G, Onoufriadis A, Palmer CN, Perricone C, Pirinen M, Plomin R, Potter SC, Pujol RM, Rautanen A, Riveira-Munoz E, Ryan AW, Salmhofer W, Samuelsson L, Sawcer SJ, Schalkwijk J, Smith CH, Ståhle M, Su Z, Tazi-Ahnini R, Traupe H, Viswanathan AC, Warren RB, Weger W, Wolk K, Wood N, Worthington J, Young HS, Zeeuwen PL, Hayday A, Burden AD, Griffiths CE, Kere J, Reis A, McVean G, Evans DM, Brown MA, Barker JN, Peltonen L, Donnelly P, Trembath RC | title = A genome-wide association study identifies new psoriasis susceptibility loci and an interaction between HLA-C and ERAP1 | journal = Nature Genetics | volume = 42 | issue = 11 | pages = 985–990 | date = November 2010 | pmid = 20953190 | pmc = 3749730 | doi = 10.1038/ng.694 }}</ref> In these conditions, ''ERAP1'' is often in [[epistasis]] with the primary risk MHC-I [[allele]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = López de Castro JA, Alvarez-Navarro C, Brito A, Guasp P, Martín-Esteban A, Sanz-Bravo A | title = Molecular and pathogenic effects of endoplasmic reticulum aminopeptidases ERAP1 and ERAP2 in MHC-I-associated inflammatory disorders: Towards a unifying view | journal = Molecular Immunology | volume = 77 | pages = 193–204 | date = September 2016 | pmid = 27522479 | doi = 10.1016/j.molimm.2016.08.005 }}</ref> Other disease associations include insulin dependent [[Diabetes|Diabetes Mellitus]] and [[Multiple sclerosis|Multiple Sclerosis]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Pepelyayeva Y, Amalfitano A | title = The role of ERAP1 in autoinflammation and autoimmunity | journal = Human Immunology | volume = 80 | issue = 5 | pages = 302–309 | date = May 2019 | pmid = 30817945 | doi = 10.1016/j.humimm.2019.02.013 }}</ref> Historically, ''ERAP1'' gene associations were first reported in [[Hypertension]].<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Yamamoto N, Nakayama J, Yamakawa-Kobayashi K, Hamaguchi H, Miyazaki R, Arinami T | title = Identification of 33 polymorphisms in the adipocyte-derived leucine aminopeptidase (ALAP) gene and possible association with hypertension | journal = Human Mutation | volume = 19 | issue = 3 | pages = 251–257 | date = March 2002 | pmid = 11857741 | doi = 10.1002/humu.10047 }}</ref> Emerging evidence links ERAP1 SNVs to cancer development, and susceptibility to infectious disease,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Cifaldi L, Romania P, Lorenzi S, Locatelli F, Fruci D | title = Role of endoplasmic reticulum aminopeptidases in health and disease: from infection to cancer | journal = International Journal of Molecular Sciences | volume = 13 | issue = 7 | pages = 8338–8352 | date = 2012-07-04 | pmid = 22942706 | pmc = 3430237 | doi = 10.3390/ijms13078338 | doi-access = free }}</ref> such as ERAP1 SNVs that modify the resistance to influenza virus infection.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Schott BH, Wang L, Zhu X, Harding AT, Ko ER, Bourgeois JS, Washington EJ, Burke TW, Anderson J, Bergstrom E, Gardener Z, Paterson S, Brennan RG, Chiu C, McClain MT, Woods CW, Gregory SG, Heaton NS, Ko DC | title = Single-cell genome-wide association reveals that a nonsynonymous variant in ''ERAP1'' confers increased susceptibility to influenza virus | journal = Cell Genomics | volume = 2 | issue = 11 | article-number = 100207 | date = November 2022 | pmid = 36465279 | pmc = 9718543 | doi = 10.1016/j.xgen.2022.100207 }}</ref>

== Structure / Mechanism ==

=== Structure ===
[[File:Figure 2. Crystal structure of ERAP1 in (A) closed conformation (PDB ID code 2YD0) and (B) open conformation (PDB ID code 3MDJ)..pdf|thumb|'''Figure 2'''. Crystal structure of ERAP1 in ('''A''') closed conformation (PDB ID code 2YD0) and ('''B''') open conformation (PDB ID code 3MDJ).|384x384px]]
ERAP1 belongs to the [[Oxytocinase|oxytocinase subfamily]] of the M1-family of zinc [[Metalloproteinase|metalloproteases]]. It is composed of four structural domains. Domain I (residues 1–254) consists of an eight-stranded ß-sheet and provides binding sites for the N-terminus of substrates. It fits against the catalytic domain II and engages with domain IV through an elongated loop. Domain II (residues 255–529) is the thermolysin-like catalytic domain, composed by an alpha-helix and a five stranded beta sheet. This sheet comprises the specific for exopeptidases GAMEN motif which creates one part of the substrate binding-cleft. The catalytic Zn atom is coordinated by the residues His353, His357 and Glu386, found in the zinc-binding motif (H-E-X-X-H-X18-E) on the helix 6a. Domain III (residues 530–614) is composed by two beta-sheets forming a beta sandwich and acts as a linker between domains II and IV. Finally, domain IV (615–941) consists mainly of a-helices and exhibits a bowl-shaped form. At the closed (active) state, it juxtaposes with domain II forming a large internal cavity, which holds the C-term substrate binding site (Figure 2A). It is the most variable domain among this family of aminopeptidases.<ref name="Kochan_2011">{{cite journal | vauthors = Kochan G, Krojer T, Harvey D, Fischer R, Chen L, Vollmar M, von Delft F, Kavanagh KL, Brown MA, Bowness P, Wordsworth P, Kessler BM, Oppermann U | title = Crystal structures of the endoplasmic reticulum aminopeptidase-1 (ERAP1) reveal the molecular basis for N-terminal peptide trimming | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 108 | issue = 19 | pages = 7745–7750 | date = May 2011 | pmid = 21508329 | pmc = 3093473 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.1101262108 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2011PNAS..108.7745K }}</ref><ref name="Nguyen_2011">{{cite journal | vauthors = Nguyen TT, Chang SC, Evnouchidou I, York IA, Zikos C, Rock KL, Goldberg AL, Stratikos E, Stern LJ | title = Structural basis for antigenic peptide precursor processing by the endoplasmic reticulum aminopeptidase ERAP1 | journal = Nature Structural & Molecular Biology | volume = 18 | issue = 5 | pages = 604–613 | date = May 2011 | pmid = 21478864 | pmc = 3087843 | doi = 10.1038/nsmb.2021 }}</ref>

=== Mechanism ===
ERAP1 uses a catalytic mechanism similar to the one proposed for LTA4 hydrolase.<ref name="Kochan_2011" /> ERAP1 adopts a thermolysinlike fold and has been crystallized in two distinct conformations: a. the open and b. the closed (Figure 2). In the open conformation, domain IV lies away from the active site thus making the internal cavity more accessible to substrates. In the closed conformation, the internal cavity is occluded from the external solvent, and it is of adequate size to accommodate a 16-residue peptide. The catalytic residues and in particular Tyr438 are optimally positioned for catalysis in the closed conformation. Consequently, substrate binding is hypothesized to take place in the open conformation, while N-terminal bond cleavage takes place in the closed one.<ref name="Kochan_2011" /><ref name="Nguyen_2011" /> It has been proposed that binding of substrate or small inhibitors induces conformational closing of ERAP1 in solution.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Maben Z, Arya R, Georgiadis D, Stratikos E, Stern LJ | title = Conformational dynamics linked to domain closure and substrate binding explain the ERAP1 allosteric regulation mechanism | journal = Nature Communications | volume = 12 | issue = 1 | article-number = 5302 | date = September 2021 | pmid = 34489420 | pmc = 8421391 | doi = 10.1038/s41467-021-25564-w | bibcode = 2021NatCo..12.5302M }}</ref>

ERAP1 prefers peptide substrates 9-16 amino acids long and is much less active for peptides 8-9 amino acids long. It is considered that ERAP1 uses a "molecular ruler" mechanism, according to which the substrate binds through its hydrophobic C-terminus in a hydrophobic pocket at the junction of domain III and domain IV and the N-terminus binds to the active site. When the length of the peptide is shorter than 8 or 9 amino acids, the peptide is too short to reach the active site, limiting rates of cleavage.<ref name="Chang_2005" />

ERAP1 has a wide substrate specificity with a preference for hydrophobic residues (e.g. leucine and methionine) at the N-terminus of the peptide substrate.<ref name="Hearn_2009">{{cite journal | vauthors = Hearn A, York IA, Rock KL | title = The specificity of trimming of MHC class I-presented peptides in the endoplasmic reticulum | journal = Journal of Immunology | volume = 183 | issue = 9 | pages = 5526–5536 | date = November 2009 | pmid = 19828632 | pmc = 2855122 | doi = 10.4049/jimmunol.0803663 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Evnouchidou I, Momburg F, Papakyriakou A, Chroni A, Leondiadis L, Chang SC, Goldberg AL, Stratikos E | title = The internal sequence of the peptide-substrate determines its N-terminus trimming by ERAP1 | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 3 | issue = 11 | article-number = e3658 | date = 2008-11-06 | pmid = 18987748 | pmc = 2573961 | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0003658 | bibcode = 2008PLoSO...3.3658E | doi-access = free | veditors = El-Shemy HA }}</ref> Tryptophan, arginine, cysteine and charged amino acids, like aspartic and glutamic acid, are poorly removed.<ref name="Nguyen_2011" /><ref name="Hearn_2009" /> ERAP1's trimming efficiency can also be influenced by the internal sequence of the peptide, with preferences for hydrophobic and positively charged residues.<ref name="Hearn_2009" />

=== Interactions ===
* [[ERP44|ERp44]]

Hisatsune et al. demonstrated, by co-immunoprecipitation, an interaction between ERAP1 and the disulfide-shuffling chaperone [[ERP44|ERp44]], facilitated by disulfide bonds formed with cysteine residues in the exon 10 loop of ERAP1.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Hisatsune C, Ebisui E, Usui M, Ogawa N, Suzuki A, Mataga N, Takahashi-Iwanaga H, Mikoshiba K | title = ERp44 Exerts Redox-Dependent Control of Blood Pressure at the ER | journal = Molecular Cell | volume = 58 | issue = 6 | pages = 1015–1027 | date = June 2015 | pmid = 25959394 | doi = 10.1016/j.molcel.2015.04.008 | doi-access = free }}</ref> This interaction was proposed to be the main mechanism for ER retention.

* Heterodimerization with [[ERAP2]]

Some experimental evidence has indicated the possibility of heterodimer formation between ERAP1 and ERAP2, another member of the oxytocinase sub-family of M1 aminopeptidases, that shares structural and functional similarities. The co-elution of ERAP1 and ERAP2 was detected through microsome fractionation, in the 230 kDa fraction, suggesting the formation of heterodimers.<ref name="Saveanu_2005">{{cite journal | vauthors = Saveanu L, Carroll O, Lindo V, Del Val M, Lopez D, Lepelletier Y, Greer F, Schomburg L, Fruci D, Niedermann G, van Endert PM | title = Concerted peptide trimming by human ERAP1 and ERAP2 aminopeptidase complexes in the endoplasmic reticulum | journal = Nature Immunology | volume = 6 | issue = 7 | pages = 689–697 | date = July 2005 | pmid = 15908954 | doi = 10.1038/ni1208 }}</ref> [[Proximity ligation assay]] analysis suggested a direct physical interaction between the two enzymes.<ref name="Saveanu_2005" /> A leucine zipper mediated ERAP1/ERAP2 complex exhibited enhanced trimming efficiency compared to a mixture of the two enzymes.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Evnouchidou I, Weimershaus M, Saveanu L, van Endert P | title = ERAP1-ERAP2 dimerization increases peptide-trimming efficiency | journal = Journal of Immunology | volume = 193 | issue = 2 | pages = 901–908 | date = July 2014 | pmid = 24928998 | doi = 10.4049/jimmunol.1302855 }}</ref> Computational dynamics showed that ERAP1/ERAP2 heterodimerization could be mediated by the exon 10 loop,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Papakyriakou A, Mpakali A, Stratikos E | title = Can ERAP1 and ERAP2 Form Functional Heterodimers? A Structural Dynamics Investigation | journal = Frontiers in Immunology | volume = 13 | article-number = 863529 | date = 2022-04-20 | pmid = 35514997 | pmc = 9065437 | doi = 10.3389/fimmu.2022.863529 | doi-access = free }}</ref> known to be involved in ERAP1-ERp44 interactions.

* MHC I proposed interaction

Chen et al. suggested that ERAP1 can trim N-terminally extended precursor antigenic peptides when bound onto MHC I.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Chen H, Li L, Weimershaus M, Evnouchidou I, van Endert P, Bouvier M | title = ERAP1-ERAP2 dimers trim MHC I-bound precursor peptides; implications for understanding peptide editing | journal = Scientific Reports | volume = 6 | issue = 1 | article-number = 28902 | date = August 2016 | pmid = 27514473 | pmc = 4981824 | doi = 10.1038/srep28902 | bibcode = 2016NatSR...628902C }}</ref> However, a re-evaluation of this trimming model by kinetic and biochemical analyses suggested that most MHC-I bound peptides had limited to no access to the active site of ERAP.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Mavridis G, Arya R, Domnick A, Zoidakis J, Makridakis M, Vlahou A, Mpakali A, Lelis A, Georgiadis D, Tampé R, Papakyriakou A, Stern LJ, Stratikos E | title = A systematic re-examination of processing of MHCI-bound antigenic peptide precursors by endoplasmic reticulum aminopeptidase 1 | journal = The Journal of Biological Chemistry | volume = 295 | issue = 21 | pages = 7193–7210 | date = May 2020 | pmid = 32184355 | pmc = 7247305 | doi = 10.1074/jbc.RA120.012976 | doi-access = free }}</ref>

== Therapeutic approaches and pharmacology ==
Therapeutic approaches for ERAP1 regulation rely mostly on the development of small molecule inhibitors. The most explored classes of inhibitors for ERAP1 are the catalytic or the allosteric site ones.[[File:Fig.2D chemical structure of DG046.pdf|thumb|'''Figure 3'''. 2D chemical structure of DG046.|284x284px]]

=== ERAP1 catalytic site inhibitors ===
* Phosphinic derivatives
The first generation of  ERAP1 inhibitors is a series of phosphinic pseudopeptides derived from a rational design approach targeting the catalytic zinc-binding site, in 2013<sup>1</sup>. Notably, DG013A (Table 1, Figure 3) displayed high potency (ERAP1 IC<sub>50</sub> = 33 nM) but poor selectivity against ERAP2 and IRAP, with reported SAR optimization studies demonstrating the importance of side chains at positions P<sub>1</sub>' and P<sub>2</sub>'.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Zervoudi E, Saridakis E, Birtley JR, Seregin SS, Reeves E, Kokkala P, Aldhamen YA, Amalfitano A, Mavridis IM, James E, Georgiadis D, Stratikos E | title = Rationally designed inhibitor targeting antigen-trimming aminopeptidases enhances antigen presentation and cytotoxic T-cell responses | journal = Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America | volume = 110 | issue = 49 | pages = 19890–19895 | date = December 2013 | pmid = 24248368 | pmc = 3856834 | doi = 10.1073/pnas.1309781110 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2013PNAS..11019890Z }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Kokkala P, Mpakali A, Mauvais FX, Papakyriakou A, Daskalaki I, Petropoulou I, Kavvalou S, Papathanasopoulou M, Agrotis S, Fonsou TM, van Endert P, Stratikos E, Georgiadis D | title = Optimization and Structure-Activity Relationships of Phosphinic Pseudotripeptide Inhibitors of Aminopeptidases That Generate Antigenic Peptides | journal = Journal of Medicinal Chemistry | volume = 59 | issue = 19 | pages = 9107–9123 | date = October 2016 | pmid = 27606717 | doi = 10.1021/acs.jmedchem.6b01031 }}</ref> A high-resolution crystal structure of phosphinic analogue DG046 bound in the active site of ERAP1, has been obtained (Figure 4).<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Giastas P, Neu M, Rowland P, Stratikos E | title = High-Resolution Crystal Structure of Endoplasmic Reticulum Aminopeptidase 1 with Bound Phosphinic Transition-State Analogue Inhibitor | journal = ACS Medicinal Chemistry Letters | volume = 10 | issue = 5 | pages = 708–713 | date = May 2019 | pmid = 31097987 | pmc = 6511960 | doi = 10.1021/acsmedchemlett.9b00002 }}</ref>

* DABA analogues

A novel family of zinc-targeting diaminobenzoic acid (DABA) compounds were rationally designed and developed in 2013, displaying micromolar potency for ERAP1 inhibition (compound 2, IC<sub>50</sub> = 2 μM, Table 1). Moderate selectivity over ERAP2 and IRAP with additional optimization efforts based on extensive investigation of SAR has been achieved.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Papakyriakou A, Zervoudi E, Theodorakis EA, Saveanu L, Stratikos E, Vourloumis D | title = Novel selective inhibitors of aminopeptidases that generate antigenic peptides | journal = Bioorganic & Medicinal Chemistry Letters | volume = 23 | issue = 17 | pages = 4832–4836 | date = September 2013 | pmid = 23916253 | doi = 10.1016/j.bmcl.2013.07.024 | url = https://escholarship.org/uc/item/8bs4b9mj }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Papakyriakou A, Zervoudi E, Tsoukalidou S, Mauvais FX, Sfyroera G, Mastellos DC, van Endert P, Theodorakis EA, Vourloumis D, Stratikos E | title = 3,4-diaminobenzoic acid derivatives as inhibitors of the oxytocinase subfamily of M1 aminopeptidases with immune-regulating properties | journal = Journal of Medicinal Chemistry | volume = 58 | issue = 3 | pages = 1524–1543 | date = February 2015 | pmid = 25635706 | doi = 10.1021/jm501867s | url = https://escholarship.org/uc/item/4wq5n2jm }}</ref>

* Urea derivatives
[[File:Figure 4. Crystal structure of ERAP1 co-crystallized with DG046..pdf|thumb|'''Figure 4.''' Crystal structure of ERAP1 co-crystallized with DG046.|285x285px]]
In 2020, urea derivative 3 (Table 1) was identified via [[high-throughput screening]] (HTS), as a competitive inhibitor of ERAP1 aminopeptidase activity (IC<sub>50</sub> = 6.9 μM) with increased selectivity over ERAP2 and IRAP.<ref name="Maben_2020">{{cite journal | vauthors = Maben Z, Arya R, Rane D, An WF, Metkar S, Hickey M, Bender S, Ali A, Nguyen TT, Evnouchidou I, Schilling R, Stratikos E, Golden J, Stern LJ | title = Discovery of Selective Inhibitors of Endoplasmic Reticulum Aminopeptidase 1 | journal = Journal of Medicinal Chemistry | volume = 63 | issue = 1 | pages = 103–121 | date = January 2020 | pmid = 31841350 | pmc = 8218592 | doi = 10.1021/acs.jmedchem.9b00293 }}</ref> SAR exploration and docking studies showed that the ''N''-acetylpiperazine carbonyl group was critical for the activity via its zinc-binding group properties.<ref name="Maben_2020" />

=== ERAP1 allosteric site inhibitors ===

* Compound GSK235

Methyl (3R,4R)-1-(3-cyano-4-methyl-6-((4-methyltetrahydro-2H-pyran-4-yl)amino)pyridin-2-yl)-4-isopropylpyrrolidine-3-carboxylate was reported to be a potent, selective and orally available inhibitor of ERAP1. In vivo experiments showed efficacy in tumor growth control in a mouse model as well as therapeutic benefit in an inflammatory autoimmunity murine model. This compound ("'''GSK235'''") is a potent ''in vivo'' tool for exploring ERAP1 biology and possible therapeutic applications.<ref name="Tinworth 2025">{{cite bioRxiv | vauthors =Tinworth CP, Wojno-Picon J, Adam M, Gade S, Hancock AP, Hirst DJ, Hutchinson JP, Kitchen SJ, Koumantou D, Lea JD, Lehmann S, Liddle J, Lonsdale R, Neu M, Nickels L, Phillipou A, Rowedder JE, Rowland P, Schneck JL, Scott-Stevens PT, Sheehan H, Steidel M, Tayler CL, Temponeras I, Thang K, Tough DF, Vitulli G, Wall ID, Young RJ, Zinn N, Peace S, Stratikos E
| title = Discovery of an orally available potent ER aminopeptidase 1 (ERAP1) inhibitor that enhances anti-tumor responses and limits inflammatory autoimmunity in vivo | biorxiv = 10.1101/2025.11.17.686761 }}</ref>

* Cyclohexyl acids

Optimization of a library screening hit has led to the identification of cyclohexyl acids with excellent potency (pIC50 = 8.6), cellular activity (pIC50 = 7.7) and moderate unbound clearance and oral bioavailability highlighting the promise of this series as drug leads.<ref name="Hryczanek 2024">{{cite journal | vauthors =Hryczanek RP, Hackett AS, Rowland P, Chung CW, Convery MA, Holmes DS, Hutchinson JP, Kitchen S, Korczynska J, Law RP, Lea JD, Liddle J, Lonsdale R, Neu M, Nickels L, Phillipou A, Rowedder JE, Schneck JL, Scott-Stevens P, Sheehan H, Tayler CL, Temponeras I, Tinworth CP, Walker AL, Wojno-Picon J, Young RJ, Lindsay DM, Stratikos E
 | title = Optimization of Potent and Selective Cyclohexyl Acid ERAP1 Inhibitors Using Structure- and Property-Based Drug Design | journal = ACS Medicinal Chemistry Letters | volume = 15 | issue = 12 | pages =2107–2114 | date = Nov 2024 | pmid = 39691536 | doi = 10.1021/acsmedchemlett.4c00401 |doi-access=free | pmc = 11647717 }}</ref>

* Clerodane acid

A high-throughput library screen identified Clerodane acid, a natural product from Dodonaea viscosa, to be a selective  activator of ERAP1 for small substrates and an inhibitor of peptide trimming. This compound was found active in blocking ERAP1-dependent antigen presentation (EC50 = 1μΜ) and to bind to the regulatory site of ERAP1 that normally accommodates C-termini of peptide substates.<ref name="Liddle 2020">{{cite journal | vauthors = Liddle J, Hutchinson JP, Kitchen S, Rowland P, Neu M, Cecconie T, Holmes DS, Jones E, Korczynska J, Koumantou D, Lea JD, Nickels L, Pemberton M, Phillipou A, Schneck JL, Sheehan H, Tinworth CP, Uings I, Wojno-Picon J, Young RJ, Stratikos E | title = Targeting the Regulatory Site of ER Aminopeptidase 1 Leads to the Discovery of a Natural Product Modulator of Antigen Presentation | journal = Journal of Medicinal Chemistry | volume = 63 | issue = 6 | pages = 3348–3358 | date = March 2020 | pmid = 32109056 | doi = 10.1021/acs.jmedchem.9b02123 }}</ref> Clerodane acid was also found to induce strong immunopeptidome shifts in cancer cells.<ref name="Temponeras 2023">{{cite journal | vauthors = Temponeras I, Samiotaki M, Koumantou D, Nikopaschou M, Kuiper J, Panayotou G, Stratikos E | title = Distinct modulation of cellular immunopeptidome by the allosteric regulatory site of ER aminopeptidase 1 | journal = European Journal of Immunology | volume = 53 | issue = 8 | article-number = e2350449 | date = Aug 2023 | pmid = 37134263 | doi = 10.1002/eji.202350449 | url = https://dspace.library.uu.nl/bitstream/handle/1874/450390/Eur_J_Immunol_-_2023_-_Temponeras_-_Distinct_modulation_of_cellular_immunopeptidome_by_the_allosteric_regulatory_site_of_ER.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y }}</ref>

* Benzofurans

This family of compounds were identified as potential allosteric (C-terminus recognition site of peptides) inhibitor via fluorescence-based high-throughput screening in 2021.<ref name="Deddouche-Grass_2021">{{cite journal | vauthors = Deddouche-Grass S, Andouche C, Bärenz F, Halter C, Hohwald A, Lebrun L, Membré N, Morales R, Muzet N, Poirot M, Reynaud M, Roujean V, Weber F, Zimmermann A, Heng R, Basse N | title = Discovery and Optimization of a Series of Benzofuran Selective ERAP1 Inhibitors: Biochemical and ''In Silico'' Studies | journal = ACS Medicinal Chemistry Letters | volume = 12 | issue = 7 | pages = 1137–1142 | date = July 2021 | pmid = 34267884 | pmc = 8274102 | doi = 10.1021/acsmedchemlett.1c00235 }}</ref> Compound 4 (Table 1) displayed high potency (ERAP1 IC<sub>50</sub> = 34 nM) and at the same time selectivity against ERAP2 and IRAP.<ref name="Deddouche-Grass_2021" />

* Sulfonamides

Sulfonamide compound 5 (Table 1) was identified through high-throughput screening studies as a potential allosteric selective inhibitor, binding at the interface between domain II and IV of ERAP1. It activates small peptide hydrolysis but effectively inhibits processing of long peptides with 8−13 residues (IC<sub>50</sub> = 5.3 μM) and displays selectivity over ERAP2 and IRAP.<ref name="Maben_2020" />

=== Clinical trials ===
As of 2023, an ERAP1 Inhibitor (GRWD5769) developed by Grey Wolf Therapeutics has entered phase I/II. Its safety, tolerability, efficacy, and pharmacokinetics are being evaluated in patients with viral associated solid tumours (head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, cervical cancer, and hepatocellular carcinoma) that are particularly sensitive to ERAP1 inhibition, as monotherapy, or in combination with PD-1 immune checkpoint Inhibitor Libtayo<sup>®</sup> (cemiplimab).<ref>{{Cite web |title=Grey Wolf Therapeutics Announces Dosing of First Patient in Phase 1/2 Clinical Study of GRWD5769 in Patients with Advanced Solid Tumours. |date=28 March 2023 |url=https://greywolftherapeutics.com/grey-wolf-therapeutics-announces-dosing-of-first-patient-in-phase-1-2-clinical-study-of-grwd5769-in-patients-with-advanced-solid-tumours/}}</ref>
[[File:Table 1 Representative examples of reported ERAP1 inhibitors.pdf|thumb|720x720px|'''Table 1.''' Representative examples of ERAP1 inhibitors. IC<sub>50</sub> value measured on long-peptide assay.|center]]
